sourceMovie.title: Sliver
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Sliver for the same reason it broke through in 1993: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Phillip Noyce keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The drama and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Eyes Wide Shut
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Sliver clicked for you, Eyes Wide Shut (1999) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Rosemary's Baby
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Sliver clicked for you, Rosemary's Baby (1968) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Sliver?
faq.answer: Try Eyes Wide Shut, Rosemary's Baby, A Walk Among the Tombstones—each shares DNA with Sliver while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Sliver?
faq.answer: Sliver (1993) is best described as Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Sliver worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 5.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
